title: NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NEURAL NETWORKS WITH TRAINABLE MATRIX ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The training process of neural networks usually optimizes weights and bias parameters of linear transformations, while nonlinear activation functions are prespecified and fixed. This work develops a systematic approach to constructing matrix activation functions whose entries are generalized from ReLU. The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons. The proposed activation functions depend on parameters that are trained along with the weights and bias vectors. Neural networks based on this approach are simple and efficient and are shown to be robust in numerical experiments.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In recent decades, deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved significant successes in many fields such as computer vision and natural language processing (Voulodimos et al., 2018), (Otter et al., 2018). The DNN surrogate model is constructed using recursive composition of linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions. To achieve good performance, it is essential to choose activation functions suitable for specific applications. In practice, Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is one of the most popular activation functions for its simplicity and efficiency. A drawback of ReLU is the presence of vanishing gradient in the training process, known as dying ReLU problem (Lu et al., 2019). Several relatively new activation approaches are proposed to overcome this problem, e.g., the simple Leaky ReLU, and Piecewise Linear Unit (PLU) (Nicolae, 2018), Softplus (Glorot et al., 2011), Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) (Clevert et al., 2016), Scaled Exponential Linear Unit (SELU) (Klambauer et al., 2017), Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2016).

Although the aforementioned activation functions are shown to be competitive in benchmark tests, they are still fixed nonlinear functions. In a DNN structure, it is often hard to determine a priori the optimal activation function for a specific application. In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using function approximation examples and well-known benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. There are a few classical works on adaptively tuning of parameters in the training process, e.g., the parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b). However, our adaptive matrix activation functions are shown to be competitive and more robust in those experiments.

## REMARK

_The activation functions used in TMAF neural network are not piecewise constants. Instead, TMAF activation is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants. There exist several trainable activation functions in the literature, e.g., Parametric ReLU (He et al., 2015b), Pad´e Activation Unit (PAU) (Molina et al., 2020), Piecewise Linear Unit (PWLU) (Zhou et al., 2021), Swish (Ramachandran et al., 2017), adaptive rational functions (Boull´e et al., 2020), adaptive splines (Bohra et al., 2020), adaptive activation functions for physics informed neural networks (ada, 2020). We point out that TMAF is different from the aforementioned activation approaches. One might think TMAF is similar to PWLU because they are both defined piecewise. However, by construction TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a_ continuous _piecewise linear function with trainable slopes._

Starting from the diagonal activation _Dℓ_ , we can go one step further to construct more general activation matrices. First we note that _Dℓ_ could be viewed as a nonlinear operator _Tℓ_ : [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ] →_ [ _C_ (R _[d]_ )] _[n][ℓ]_ , where

There seems to be no special reason, aside from keeping the computational cost as low as possible, to consider only diagonal operators _D_ . A more ambitious approach is to consider a trainable nonlinear activation _operator_ determined by more general matrices, for example, by a tri-diagonal operator

for _x ∈_ R _[d] ._ The diagonal entry _αℓ_ is given in equation 6 while the off-diagonal entries _βℓ_ , _γℓ_ are piecewise constant functions defined in a fashion similar to _αℓ_ . Theoretically speaking, even trainable full matrix activation is possible despite of potentially huge training cost. In summary, a DNN based on trainable nonlinear activation operators _{Tℓ}[L] ℓ_ =1[reads]

As we indicated above, TMAF can be used with any matrix and this will lead to a great flexibility in training and approximation. In fact, if a sparse matrix _D_ is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient. We can choose p-diagonal matrices, as well as matrices with prescribed sparsity patterns. Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.

diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are shown in Figures 7a, 7b and Table 3. We observe that performance of the TMAF and the ReLU networks are similar. Such a behavior clearly should be expected for a simple dataset such as MNIST.

For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 8a, 8b and Table 3. Those parameters given in (Paszke et al., 2019) are already tuned well with respect to ReLU. Nevertheless, TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.

For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a) with the ReLU and TMAF activation functions in Subsection 3.1. Numerical results are presented in Figures 9a and 9b. In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.

It is possible to improve the performance of TMAF applied to those benchmark datasets. The key point is to select suitable intervals in _αℓ_ to optimize the performance. A simple strategy is to let those intervals in equation 6 be varying and adjusted in the training (or the evaluation) process, which will be investigated in our future research.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a novel framework—Trainable Matrix Activation Functions (TMAF)—that generalizes conventional activation mechanisms in neural networks by representing them as matrix-vector multiplications with trainable parameters. While the core idea of learning adaptive activation functions is not entirely new, the proposed reformulation of ReLU-type activations as diagonal matrix operators with piecewise-constant entries is conceptually interesting and offers a fresh perspective on parameterization. The paper is generally well-structured, with a clear motivation rooted in the limitations of fixed activation functions like ReLU, and it situates itself within the broader context of adaptive activation methods. However, the theoretical development is somewhat shallow—particularly in how the matrix formalism enhances expressivity or optimizability beyond existing approaches—and key methodological details, such as the exact parameterization of the step function $\alpha_\ell$, the training procedure for the activation parameters, and computational complexity, are inadequately addressed. Crucially, the paper lacks a rigorous ablation study or statistical validation of its claims regarding robustness and performance gains over strong baselines like Swish, GELU, or other trainable activations; the experiments, while covering standard benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100), appear preliminary and do not conclusively demonstrate superiority in terms of accuracy, convergence speed, or generalization. Moreover, the remark distinguishing TMAF from prior work, especially PWLU, hinges on discontinuity, but fails to discuss potential downsides, such as gradient instability during training due to non-smoothness. Overall, while the paper presents a conceptually intriguing direction, it falls short in technical depth, empirical rigor, and clarity of contribution relative to the state of the art.

where m is a positive integer and {tℓ,j}[m] j =0[and] [{][s][j][}][m] j =1[are][constants.][For][the][time][being,][let] Dℓ( ηℓ( x )) ηℓ( x ) = σℓ( ηℓ( x )) with σℓ being a scalar-valued function applied to ηℓ( x ) componentwise.
comments.explanation: The passage introduces αℓ as a step function but does not explicitly define its form—no mathematical expression is given for how the constants {sj} and {tℓ,j} determine αℓ. From context, especially Figure 1 and surrounding text, we infer that αℓ is a piecewise constant function over m+1 intervals defined by break points s1 < s2 < … < sm, taking values tℓ,0, tℓ,1, ..., tℓ,m. However, the assignment of values to intervals (e.g., whether tℓ,0 applies on (−∞, s1) or (s1, ∞)) is left ambiguous. While the two examples in Figure 1 clarify two specific cases (e.g., m=1 and m=2), a general reader cannot unambiguously reconstruct αℓ’s structure for arbitrary m without assuming conventions (e.g., left-open vs. right-closed intervals). This matters because inconsistent interval handling could lead to implementation errors. The omission may stem from OCR dropping the equation (possibly labeled as 'equation 5'), but even assuming its presence, the text should minimally describe the functional mapping. The authors correctly frame the idea and examples, but the core definition is underspecified.

comments.title: Underspecification of interval selection for αℓ in experiments
comments.quote: For TMAF Dℓ in equation 5, the function αℓ uses intervals ( −∞, −1.4), ( −1.4 , −0.92], ... such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The method for setting interval boundaries based on Gaussian quantiles is described, but key implementation details are missing: Is the distribution standard normal? Is input data normalized before applying TMAF? The use of BatchNorm1d shortly after suggests internal normalization, but the interaction between BatchNorm and interval placement is not discussed. Moreover, using fixed quantiles assumes stable activation distributions, which may shift during training—yet intervals are fixed. The authors acknowledge in Section 3.3 that letting intervals adapt could improve performance, which validates this as a design compromise. However, without specifying normalization strategy or distribution assumptions, reproduction is hindered. This is particularly important because interval design directly affects the inductive bias of TMAF. While the description suffices for high-level understanding, precise replication would require additional details not provided—e.g., mean and variance of the assumed Gaussian, or whether statistics are computed from training data or initialized values.

comments.title: Misleading implication of matrix-vector cost equivalence to scalar operations
comments.quote: The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons.
comments.explanation: This sentence from the abstract implies that the proposed method relies only on operations as cheap as those in standard ReLU. However, matrix-vector multiplication (even diagonal) with trainable piecewise-constant entries requires evaluating αℓ(t) for each input component, which involves comparing t to all m break points—cost O(m) per neuron. For large m, this exceeds ReLU's single comparison. The phrase 'using only scalar multiplications and comparisons' is technically true but misleading: it omits the combinatorial cost of the step function evaluation. Standard ReLU uses one comparison; piecewise constants with m segments require up to m comparisons. The authors may assume m is small, but without bounding m, this could scale poorly. Later experiments use 100 intervals, implying significant overhead. The method remains efficient due to sparsity or implementation optimizations (e.g., binary search), but this should be acknowledged. As stated, the claim understates computational cost relative to standard activations.

comments.title: Tri-diagonal TMAF activation function not fully specified
comments.quote: The tri-diagonal TMAF is given in equation 7, where _αℓ_ is the same as the diagonal TMAF, while _βℓ_ is piecewise constant with respect to intervals ( _−∞, −_ 2 _. 01 + 99 _h_ ], ( _−_ 2 _. 01 + _kh, −_ 2 _. 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][(] _[−]_[0] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)][,][and] _[γ][ℓ]_[is][a][piecewise][constant][based][on] 99 ( _−∞,_ 0 _. 01], (0 _. 01 + _kh,_ 0 _. 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][ (2] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)
comments.explanation: The text introduces tri-diagonal TMAF based on equation 7, but equation 7 is not visible in the provided context. While the paper defines diagonal TMAF via equation 6 and the structure of D_ℓ as a diagonal matrix with entries from α_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_i), the tri-diagonal version would presumably involve off-diagonal entries β_ℓ and γ_ℓ (likely sub- and super-diagonal). The authors specify that β_ℓ and γ_ℓ are piecewise constant functions defined similarly to α_ℓ, but they do not explicitly define how these functions are used in the matrix operator T_ℓ. For example, is T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) = Tridiag(β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)), α_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)), γ_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)))? And is the activation then T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x)? Crucially, unlike the diagonal case where D_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x) correctly recovers componentwise multiplication, a matrix-vector product involving off-diagonal entries would introduce cross-neuron dependencies — meaning the activation is no longer applied neuron-by-neuron but couples inputs across units. This is a fundamental architectural change that should be formally defined. The lack of such specification makes it impossible for a reader to reproduce the tri-diagonal model. Although the general idea is described, the exact form of the nonlinear operator T_ℓ is missing, especially how β_ℓ and γ_ℓ are mapped to matrix entries and how the intervals are aligned across neurons.

comments.title: Unclear mapping of piecewise constant functions to off-diagonal matrix entries
comments.explanation: The interval definition for β_ℓ is problematic. It begins with (−∞, −2.01 + 99h] and then defines a family for k=0 to 99 of intervals (−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h]. Given h = 0.02 (from diagonal TMAF), 99h ≈ 1.98, so −2.01 + 99h ≈ −0.03. That would mean the first interval is (−∞, −0.03], and then the next intervals start at −2.01 + 0*h = −2.01 and go up to −2.01 + 100h ≈ −0.01. But this creates overlap: the interval (−2.01 + 99h, −2.01 + 100h] ≈ (−0.03, −0.01] overlaps with the tail of the first interval. Moreover, the jump from −∞ to −2.01 + kh seems discontinuous. Perhaps the intention was to define β_ℓ using a uniform grid from −2.01 to −0.01 (99 intervals of size 0.02), and the first (−∞, −2.01] and last (−0.01, ∞) as extensions. But the current phrasing suggests a gap between −0.03 and −2.01, and overlap near −0.03 to −0.01. This likely stems from OCR or formatting issues: the symbol '' suggests a corrupted character, possibly a bracket or comma. Even if the intervals are meant to cover (−∞, −2.01], then [−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h) for k=0..98, and [−0.01, ∞), the current description is imprecise. Since β_ℓ is used to define off-diagonal entries, which depend on input values, the input domain partitioning must be clearly defined to avoid undefined behavior or overlap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in how matrix activation applies to vector input
comments.quote: TMAF is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants.
comments.explanation: The core definition of TMAF relies on σ_ℓ ◦ η_ℓ(x) = D_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x), where D_ℓ(y) is diagonal with entries α_ℓ(y_i). This preserves the componentwise nature of activation: each output depends only on the corresponding input. However, when generalizing to non-diagonal matrices (e.g., tri-diagonal), the operation T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x) would produce outputs that depend on multiple components of η_ℓ(x) — for instance, the i-th output could depend on η_ℓ(x)_i, η_ℓ(x)_{i−1}, and η_ℓ(x)_{i+1}. While this increases expressiveness, it departs fundamentally from the standard feedforward layer design, where activations are pointwise. The paper does not clarify whether the operator T_ℓ is applied elementwise to the vector or whether the matrix depends on the entire vector. Specifically: is T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) a matrix whose entries depend on the full vector η_ℓ(x), or is it a local operator? If the former, the model introduces global dependencies without justification; if the latter, how are the off-diagonal functions β_ℓ and γ_ℓ evaluated? For example, is the (i,i−1)-th entry β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_i) or β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_{i−1})? This is critical for reproducibility but left underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent neuron count between MNIST description and typical setup
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764)
comments.explanation: MNIST images are 28x28, so flattened input dimension is 784, not 764. The stated _n_0 = 764 appears inconsistent with standard MNIST preprocessing. I first considered whether this was an OCR error (e.g., '8' misread as '6'), but '764' appears twice in the passage in different sections (including the reference to Figure 7), making random OCR error less likely. The paper does not mention any subsampling or dimensionality reduction. While it is possible the authors used a preprocessed version of MNIST, this deviation from standard practice should be noted. The error affects reproducibility. Since the result in Table 3 shows only a 0.4% accuracy improvement with TMAF, the input dimension may not drastically change conclusions, but the specific design choice remains underspecified. This could mislead readers attempting to replicate the experiment.

comments.title: Missing loss value for Para-ReLU in Table 2
comments.quote:
||fnal loss|
|---|---|
|ReLU|1.00|
|Para ReLU|1.00|
|DiagTMAF|6.45e-2|
|Tri-diagTMAF|5.81e-2|
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports 'final loss' for ReLU, Para-ReLU, DiagTMAF, and Tri-diagTMAF. However, ReLU and Para-ReLU both have loss = 1.00, which is suspiciously identical. Given that Para-ReLU is adaptive, one would expect slightly different behavior. More importantly, the context indicates the ℓ² loss is used (in Section 3), and Figure 4b shows diverging curves for ReLU and Para-ReLU, suggesting they do not converge to the same loss value. The identical values may be a reporting error, or an artifact of early stopping or floating-point rounding. However, without more precise values, it's impossible to judge if Para-ReLU actually learns anything beyond ReLU in this task. The paper claims Para-ReLU approximates only the low-frequency component, but identical final loss contradicts this unless the approximation quality is similar. This undermines the comparison. The OCR check is clean—'1.00' appears twice—so it's likely a real reporting issue.

comments.title: Overstatement of TMAF superiority in classification without test accuracy for CIFAR-100
comments.quote: In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The claim 'TMAF again outperforms' suggests consistent superiority in evaluation, but Table 3 does not include results for CIFAR-100—only MNIST and CIFAR-10 are tabulated. While Figures 9a and 9b are mentioned, the passage does not report actual accuracy numbers for CIFAR-100. The word 'outperforms' is applied to the training process, but no test/evaluation accuracy is given for CIFAR-100 to justify general superiority. The prior successes on CIFAR-10 and MNIST are valid, but extending the claim to CIFAR-100 without data overstates what is empirically shown. A careful reader might assume the figures contain the data, but figures alone are insufficient for precise comparison. This is a questionable claim because it implies broader validation than is presented.

comments.title: Overstatement of TMAF superiority in classification without statistical significance or variance reporting
comments.quote: TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.
comments.explanation: The claim that TMAF 'returns better classification results' is based solely on point estimates in Table 3 (e.g., 80.2% vs 77.5% for CIFAR-10). However, no confidence intervals, standard deviations, or number of trials are reported. In deep learning experiments, performance can vary significantly across runs due to random initialization and data shuffling. Reporting single-run results risks overclaiming performance differences, especially for improvements of ~2-3%. While the trend across datasets is consistent (TMAF > ReLU), the lack of statistical rigor makes it hard to assess whether the improvement is robust. The authors acknowledge these are standard benchmarks and their method is competitive, but the phrasing 'still produces better results' implies generality and reproducibility that isn't fully supported. This weakens the validity of the comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistency in parameter counting between TMAF and ReLU networks
comments.quote: On the other hand, increasing the number of intervals in TMAF only lead to a few more training parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that TMAF adds few parameters compared to increasing neuron count in ReLU networks. This is generally valid—adding intervals increases m per layer, adding O(m·n_ℓ) trainable t_{ℓ,j} values, while adding neurons increases weight matrix size quadratically. However, in practice, the experiments are not directly comparable in scale. For example, in Section 3.2, diagonal TMAF uses 3 hidden layers with 400 neurons, while ReLU networks are not specified to use the same architecture. The text says 'we also consider' ReLU with 3 layers but doesn't confirm if it uses 400 neurons or a smaller number. Similarly, tri-diag TMAF uses 300 neurons. If ReLU networks used fewer neurons, the comparison becomes unfair. The claim of TMAF efficiency is sound in theory, but without matched parameter counts or FLOPs, the empirical superiority could be due to larger model size. This undermines the fairness of the comparison, though not the theoretical argument.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about ReLU failing high-frequency approximation without parameter matching
comments.quote: To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that ReLU fails on high-frequency functions unless much larger networks are used. This is a known result in neural network theory (e.g., spectral bias). However, in their experiment, the comparison is not fair in terms of capacity: the TMAF networks use 3 layers with 300–400 neurons, while the ReLU network’s size is not explicitly stated in Section 3.2 (though implied to be 'traditional' or standard). If the ReLU network is smaller, then its failure is expected due to insufficient capacity, not inherent limitation. To fairly claim that ReLU 'fails' while TMAF 'succeeds', the architectures should be matched in total parameter count or FLOPs. Otherwise, the performance gap may be due to model size, not activation expressiveness. While the point about spectral bias is valid, the empirical justification is weak without capacity matching. This overstates the demonstrated result.

comments.title: Missing specification of activation function usage in ResNet variants
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure... with ReLU and diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The authors state they replace ReLU with TMAF in ResNet18 and ResNet34. However, ResNets have multiple activation sites (e.g., after each convolution, within residual blocks). It's unclear whether TMAF replaces all ReLUs or only certain ones. Moreover, TMAF was defined for fully connected layers with vector-valued activation, but ResNets operate on tensor-valued feature maps. The method for applying TMAF to spatial dimensions (e.g., channel-wise, pixel-wise, shared across spatial dims) is not specified. BatchNorm1d is mentioned for fully connected layers, but ResNets typically use BatchNorm2d. The lack of detail on how TMAF integrates into convolutional architectures makes the method underspecified and non-reproducible. While one might assume channel-wise diagonal application, the sparsity structure and normalization compatibility are not clarified. This is a real gap in method description.

comments.title: Claim of discontinuous activation not clearly tied to TMAF generalization
comments.quote: TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a continuous piecewise linear function with trainable slopes.
comments.explanation: The remark contrasts TMAF with PWLU by emphasizing that TMAF can represent discontinuous activations, citing Figure 1. Looking at the caption, case (a) is m=1, s_1=0, t_{ℓ,0}>0, t_{ℓ,1}=1, implying α_ℓ(t) = t_{ℓ,0} for t < 0 and 1 for t ≥ 0. So σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t) t is: for t<0, t_{ℓ,0}*t; for t≥0, 1*t = t. If t_{ℓ,0} ≠ 1, this is discontinuous at 0 only if α_ℓ(t) were constant, but σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)t is continuous at 0 since both sides approach 0. For example, lim_{t→0⁻} σ_ℓ(t) = t_{ℓ,0} * 0 = 0, lim_{t→0⁺} = 1*0 = 0. So σ_ℓ(t) is continuous everywhere even if α_ℓ is discontinuous. The discontinuity in α_ℓ does not induce a jump in σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)t because t=0 at the breakpoint. The only way to get a discontinuous σ_ℓ is if there were a constant offset in α_ℓ, but α_ℓ is scaling t, not adding. So σ_ℓ(t) is always continuous at breakpoints. Figure 1 likely shows apparent 'jumps' in the slope (kinks), not actual discontinuities. The authors may mean piecewise *differentiable* or have *discontinuous derivatives*, not discontinuous values. This misleads the reader into thinking TMAF supports jump discontinuities, which it does not under the current formulation. This overstates a theoretical advantage over PWLU, which typically also produces continuous outputs. The claim is therefore questionable.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript usage in grid points {sj} vs {tℓ,j}
comments.quote: we will choose the grid points {sj}[m] j =1[a priori and train the step values [ ∪][L] ℓ =1 [{][t][ℓ,j][}][m] j =0 of αℓ .
comments.explanation: The grid points {sj} are defined without dependence on the layer index ℓ, implying a shared partition across layers. However, the step values {tℓ,j} are layer-specific, indexed by ℓ. This is reasonable and likely intentional—to fix break points but allow adaptive responses per layer. The issue arises in the notation: {sj} suggests global breakpoints, but the text never explicitly justifies this design choice. In contrast, later experiments (e.g., Section 3.1) describe intervals like (−∞, −1.4), etc., which appear layer-independent, supporting the interpretation. However, the lack of comment on why intervals are shared (rather than trainable or layer-adaptive) may mislead readers into thinking it's an oversight. Given that the authors state they fix {sj} a priori for efficiency, this is a design decision, not an error. Still, the asymmetry in indexing (ℓ in tℓ,j but not in sj) is potentially confusing and warrants clarification to avoid assumptions of typo or inconsistency.

comments.title: Overstatement of generality in 'arbitrary trainable matrix activation'
comments.quote: In this paper, we shall generalize ReLU and introduce arbitrary trainable matrix activation functions.
comments.explanation: The term 'arbitrary' suggests a universal class of matrix operators, but the paper only explores diagonal and tri-diagonal cases. Theoretical mention is made of full matrices being 'possible despite... huge training cost', but no experiments or constructions are given. While the idea is extensible, claiming 'arbitrary' may overstate current contribution. However, the subsequent qualification—'we call the neural network in equation 4... TMAF'—narrows the scope to the defined construction. In context, 'arbitrary' likely means 'beyond scalar parameter tuning' rather than literal universality. Still, it risks overclaim, especially as full-matrix activation is neither trained nor analyzed. Given that the paper focuses on sparse, structured matrices for efficiency, the word 'arbitrary' should be softened to 'structured' or 'generalized' to avoid misinterpretation.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'Figure 4b' for both exact function and training loss
comments.quote: see Figures 5 and 4b) while ReLU and parametric ReLU are not able to approximate the highly oscillating function within reasonable accuracy, see Figure 4b and Table 2.
comments.explanation: The text says ReLU and parametric ReLU perform poorly, citing Figure 4b. However, Figure 4 is captioned as having (a) 'Exact oscillating function' and (b) 'Training loss comparison'. Thus, 'Figure 4b' refers to the training loss plot, not the approximation quality. The claim about poor approximation should be supported by Figure 6, which actually shows the ReLU-type approximations. While a reader might infer that poor training loss correlates with poor approximation, this is not direct evidence. The paper would be clearer if it referenced Figure 6 directly when discussing the quality of the approximation. The issue is not a mathematical error but a misleading reference that could cause a reader to believe Figure 4b shows functional output when it only shows loss. Context helps—Figures 5 and 6 clearly contrast TMAF vs. ReLU approximations—but the pairing of 'approximate' with 'Figure 4b' still risks misinterpretation.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a novel framework—Trainable Matrix Activation Functions (TMAF)—that generalizes conventional activation mechanisms in neural networks by representing them as matrix-vector multiplications with trainable parameters. While the core idea of learning adaptive activation functions is not entirely new, the proposed reformulation of ReLU-type activations as diagonal matrix operators with piecewise-constant entries is conceptually interesting and offers a fresh perspective on parameterization. The paper is generally well-structured, with a clear motivation rooted in the limitations of fixed activation functions like ReLU, and it situates itself within the broader context of adaptive activation methods. However, the theoretical development is somewhat shallow—particularly in how the matrix formalism enhances expressivity or optimizability beyond existing approaches—and key methodological details, such as the exact parameterization of the step function $\alpha_\ell$, the training procedure for the activation parameters, and computational complexity, are inadequately addressed. Crucially, the paper lacks a rigorous ablation study or statistical validation of its claims regarding robustness and performance gains over strong baselines like Swish, GELU, or other trainable activations; the experiments, while covering standard benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100), appear preliminary and do not conclusively demonstrate superiority in terms of accuracy, convergence speed, or generalization. Moreover, the remark distinguishing TMAF from prior work, especially PWLU, hinges on discontinuity, but fails to discuss potential downsides, such as gradient instability during training due to non-smoothness. Overall, while the paper presents a conceptually intriguing direction, it falls short in technical depth, empirical rigor, and clarity of contribution relative to the state of the art.

comments.title: Potential confusion between σℓ and Dℓ in activation realization
comments.quote: let Dℓ( ηℓ( x )) ηℓ( x ) = σℓ( ηℓ( x )) with σℓ being a scalar-valued function applied to ηℓ( x ) componentwise.
comments.explanation: The equality Dℓ(ηℓ(x)) ηℓ(x) = σℓ(ηℓ(x)) equates a matrix-vector product (left) with a componentwise scalar activation (right). This only holds if Dℓ is diagonal and σℓ is such that σℓ(z) = diag(Dℓ(z)) ⊙ z. But the text calls σℓ a scalar function applied componentwise, which is standard, while Dℓ is a matrix-valued function. The claim is mathematically valid only if σℓ(t) = αℓ(t) ⋅ t, where αℓ(t) ∈ {0,1} for ReLU. However, the text does not explicitly define σℓ in terms of αℓ—only that the output equals σℓ(ηℓ(x)). This creates ambiguity: is σℓ the effective activation induced by Dℓ, or is Dℓ constructed from σℓ? The remark correctly notes that the entries of Dℓ are piecewise constant (trainable), not the activation itself. So the authors’ intent is clear: σℓ emerges from the matrix multiplication. But the phrasing 'let Dℓ(ηℓ(x)) ηℓ(x) = σℓ(ηℓ(x))' risks suggesting σℓ is independently defined. A more precise phrasing would be 'define σℓ(t) := αℓ(t) ⋅ t', making the dependency explicit. As is, careful readers can infer correctness from context, but confusion is possible.

comments.title: Misleading implication of matrix-vector cost equivalence to scalar operations
comments.quote: The activation is based on matrix-vector multiplications using only scalar multiplications and comparisons.
comments.explanation: This sentence from the abstract implies that the proposed method relies only on operations as cheap as those in standard ReLU. However, matrix-vector multiplication (even diagonal) with trainable piecewise-constant entries requires evaluating αℓ(t) for each input component, which involves comparing t to all m break points—cost O(m) per neuron. For large m, this exceeds ReLU's single comparison. The phrase 'using only scalar multiplications and comparisons' is technically true but misleading: it omits the combinatorial cost of the step function evaluation. Standard ReLU uses one comparison; piecewise constants with m segments require up to m comparisons. The authors may assume m is small, but without bounding m, this could scale poorly. Later experiments use 100 intervals, implying significant overhead. The method remains efficient due to sparsity or implementation optimizations (e.g., binary search), but this should be acknowledged. As stated, the claim understates computational cost relative to standard activations.

comments.title: Tri-diagonal TMAF activation function not fully specified
comments.quote: The tri-diagonal TMAF is given in equation 7, where _αℓ_ is the same as the diagonal TMAF, while _βℓ_ is piecewise constant with respect to intervals ( _−∞, −_ 2 _._ 01 + 99 _h_ ], ( _−_ 2 _._ 01 + _kh, −_ 2 _._ 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][(] _[−]_[0] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)][,][and] _[γ][ℓ]_[is][a][piecewise][constant][based][on] 99 ( _−∞,_ 0 _._ 01], (0 _._ 01 + _kh,_ 0 _._ 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][ (2] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)
comments.explanation: The text introduces tri-diagonal TMAF based on equation 7, but equation 7 is not visible in the provided context. While the paper defines diagonal TMAF via equation 6 and the structure of D_ℓ as a diagonal matrix with entries from α_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_i), the tri-diagonal version would presumably involve off-diagonal entries β_ℓ and γ_ℓ (likely sub- and super-diagonal). The authors specify that β_ℓ and γ_ℓ are piecewise constant functions defined similarly to α_ℓ, but they do not explicitly define how these functions are used in the matrix operator T_ℓ. For example, is T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) = Tridiag(β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)), α_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)), γ_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)))? And is the activation then T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x)? Crucially, unlike the diagonal case where D_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x) correctly recovers componentwise multiplication, a matrix-vector product involving off-diagonal entries would introduce cross-neuron dependencies — meaning the activation is no longer applied neuron-by-neuron but couples inputs across units. This is a fundamental architectural change that should be formally defined. The lack of such specification makes it impossible for a reader to reproduce the tri-diagonal model. Although the general idea is described, the exact form of the nonlinear operator T_ℓ is missing, especially how β_ℓ and γ_ℓ are mapped to matrix entries and how the intervals are aligned across neurons.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of _α_ vs _αℓ_ in tri-diagonal TMAF description
comments.quote: The diagonal TMAF uses _α_ = _αℓ_ in equation 6...
comments.explanation: The paper consistently uses α_ℓ as a layer-specific function in prior definitions (e.g., in equation 6, α_ℓ(t) = sum_{j=1}^m t_{ℓ,j−1} · 1_{[s_{j−1}, s_j)}(t)). However, in this sentence, the notation switches to _α_ without a subscript, then equates it to _αℓ_. While this might seem minor, it introduces ambiguity: is _α_ a universal function shared across layers, or is it just a typo in typesetting? The equation reference (equation 6) still defines α_ℓ with subscript ℓ, so the equality _α = αℓ_ is only meaningful if ℓ is fixed. The use of unsubscripted _α_ may be due to OCR error (e.g., missing ℓ), but even if intentional, it risks implying that the same activation parameters are reused across layers, which contradicts the earlier claim that each layer can have its own trainable t_{ℓ,j}. The text later correctly reverts to α_ℓ in reference to tri-diagonal TMAF. Given that the intent appears to be layer-specific α_ℓ, this is likely a notational slip rather than a technical error, but it momentarily disrupts clarity.

comments.title: Unclear mapping of piecewise constant functions to off-diagonal matrix entries
comments.explanation: The interval definition for β_ℓ is problematic. It begins with (−∞, −2.01 + 99h] and then defines a family for k=0 to 99 of intervals (−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h]. Given h = 0.02 (from diagonal TMAF), 99h ≈ 1.98, so −2.01 + 99h ≈ −0.03. That would mean the first interval is (−∞, −0.03], and then the next intervals start at −2.01 + 0*h = −2.01 and go up to −2.01 + 100h ≈ −0.01. But this creates overlap: the interval (−2.01 + 99h, −2.01 + 100h] ≈ (−0.03, −0.01] overlaps with the tail of the first interval. Moreover, the jump from −∞ to −2.01 + kh seems discontinuous. Perhaps the intention was to define β_ℓ using a uniform grid from −2.01 to −0.01 (99 intervals of size 0.02), and the first (−∞, −2.01] and last (−0.01, ∞) as extensions. But the current phrasing suggests a gap between −0.03 and −2.01, and overlap near −0.03 to −0.01. This likely stems from OCR or formatting issues: the symbol '' suggests a corrupted character, possibly a bracket or comma. Even if the intervals are meant to cover (−∞, −2.01], then [−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h) for k=0..98, and [−0.01, ∞), the current description is imprecise. Since β_ℓ is used to define off-diagonal entries, which depend on input values, the input domain partitioning must be clearly defined to avoid undefined behavior or overlap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in how matrix activation applies to vector input
comments.quote: TMAF is realized using matrix-vector multiplication, where entries of those matrices are trainable piecewise constants.
comments.explanation: The core definition of TMAF relies on σ_ℓ ◦ η_ℓ(x) = D_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x), where D_ℓ(y) is diagonal with entries α_ℓ(y_i). This preserves the componentwise nature of activation: each output depends only on the corresponding input. However, when generalizing to non-diagonal matrices (e.g., tri-diagonal), the operation T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) η_ℓ(x) would produce outputs that depend on multiple components of η_ℓ(x) — for instance, the i-th output could depend on η_ℓ(x)_i, η_ℓ(x)_{i−1}, and η_ℓ(x)_{i+1}. While this increases expressiveness, it departs fundamentally from the standard feedforward layer design, where activations are pointwise. The paper does not clarify whether the operator T_ℓ is applied elementwise to the vector or whether the matrix depends on the entire vector. Specifically: is T_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)) a matrix whose entries depend on the full vector η_ℓ(x), or is it a local operator? If the former, the model introduces global dependencies without justification; if the latter, how are the off-diagonal functions β_ℓ and γ_ℓ evaluated? For example, is the (i,i−1)-th entry β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_i) or β_ℓ(η_ℓ(x)_{i−1})? This is critical for reproducibility but left underspecified.

comments.title: Claim of discontinuous activation not clearly tied to TMAF generalization
comments.quote: TMAF includes discontinuous functions as special cases (see Figure 1) while PWLU always results in a continuous piecewise linear function with trainable slopes.
comments.explanation: The remark contrasts TMAF with PWLU by emphasizing that TMAF can represent discontinuous activations, citing Figure 1. Looking at the caption, case (a) is m=1, s_1=0, t_{ℓ,0}>0, t_{ℓ,1}=1, implying α_ℓ(t) = t_{ℓ,0} for t < 0 and 1 for t ≥ 0. So σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t) t is: for t<0, t_{ℓ,0}*t; for t≥0, 1*t = t. If t_{ℓ,0} ≠ 1, this is discontinuous at 0 only if α_ℓ(t) were constant, but σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)t is continuous at 0 since both sides approach 0. For example, lim_{t→0⁻} σ_ℓ(t) = t_{ℓ,0} * 0 = 0, lim_{t→0⁺} = 1*0 = 0. So σ_ℓ(t) is continuous everywhere even if α_ℓ is discontinuous. The discontinuity in α_ℓ does not induce a jump in σ_ℓ(t) = α_ℓ(t)t because t=0 at the breakpoint. The only way to get a discontinuous σ_ℓ is if there were a constant offset in α_ℓ, but α_ℓ is scaling t, not adding. So σ_ℓ(t) is always continuous at breakpoints. Figure 1 likely shows apparent 'jumps' in the slope (kinks), not actual discontinuities. The authors may mean piecewise *differentiable* or have *discontinuous derivatives*, not discontinuous values. This misleads the reader into thinking TMAF supports jump discontinuities, which it does not under the current formulation. This overstates a theoretical advantage over PWLU, which typically also produces continuous outputs. The claim is therefore questionable.

comments.title: Missing definition of loss function for classification tasks in equation form
comments.quote: The cross entropy loss function of _ηL,W,B_ then is defined by
comments.explanation: The text states that the cross-entropy loss is defined but does not provide an equation. While cross-entropy is standard, its exact form in this context matters: is it applied to softmax outputs? The paper defines η_{L,W,B} as the final layer output, which in classification should be logits. The cross-entropy loss would typically be CE = −∑_j log(softmax(x_j)_{c_j}). But without stating this, and given that the model modifies the activation structure, a reader might wonder if the loss is applied before or after TMAF at the output layer. Moreover, the missing equation is especially problematic because the TMAF activation is applied in hidden layers, but the treatment of the final layer is not discussed. Does the output layer use TMAF or softmax? The absence of a formal definition weakens methodological clarity, especially since the numerical results claim improved performance on CIFAR. Although experts can infer the likely form, the omission makes the method underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'Figure 4b' for both exact function and training loss
comments.quote: see Figures 5 and 4b) while ReLU and parametric ReLU are not able to approximate the highly oscillating function within reasonable accuracy, see Figure 4b and Table 2.
comments.explanation: The text says ReLU and parametric ReLU perform poorly, citing Figure 4b. However, Figure 4 is captioned as having (a) 'Exact oscillating function' and (b) 'Training loss comparison'. Thus, 'Figure 4b' refers to the training loss plot, not the approximation quality. The claim about poor approximation should be supported by Figure 6, which actually shows the ReLU-type approximations. While a reader might infer that poor training loss correlates with poor approximation, this is not direct evidence. The paper would be clearer if it referenced Figure 6 directly when discussing the quality of the approximation. The issue is not a mathematical error but a misleading reference that could cause a reader to believe Figure 4b shows functional output when it only shows loss. Context helps—Figures 5 and 6 clearly contrast TMAF vs. ReLU approximations—but the pairing of 'approximate' with 'Figure 4b' still risks misinterpretation.

comments.title: Inconsistent neuron count between MNIST description and typical setup
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer _n_ 0 = 764)
comments.explanation: MNIST images are 28x28, so flattened input dimension is 784, not 764. The stated _n_0 = 764 appears inconsistent with standard MNIST preprocessing. I first considered whether this was an OCR error (e.g., '8' misread as '6'), but '764' appears twice in the passage in different sections (including the reference to Figure 7), making random OCR error less likely. The paper does not mention any subsampling or dimensionality reduction. While it is possible the authors used a preprocessed version of MNIST, this deviation from standard practice should be noted. The error affects reproducibility. Since the result in Table 3 shows only a 0.4% accuracy improvement with TMAF, the input dimension may not drastically change conclusions, but the specific design choice remains underspecified. This could mislead readers attempting to replicate the experiment.

comments.title: Overstatement of TMAF superiority in classification without test accuracy for CIFAR-100
comments.quote: In the training process, TMAF again outperforms the classical ReLU network.
comments.explanation: The claim 'TMAF again outperforms' suggests consistent superiority in evaluation, but Table 3 does not include results for CIFAR-100—only MNIST and CIFAR-10 are tabulated. While Figures 9a and 9b are mentioned, the passage does not report actual accuracy numbers for CIFAR-100. The word 'outperforms' is applied to the training process, but no test/evaluation accuracy is given for CIFAR-100 to justify general superiority. The prior successes on CIFAR-10 and MNIST are valid, but extending the claim to CIFAR-100 without data overstates what is empirically shown. A careful reader might assume the figures contain the data, but figures alone are insufficient for precise comparison. This is a questionable claim because it implies broader validation than is presented.

comments.title: Missing loss value for Para-ReLU in Table 2
comments.quote:
||fnal loss|
|---|---|
|ReLU|1.00|
|Para ReLU|1.00|
|DiagTMAF|6.45e-2|
|Tri-diagTMAF|5.81e-2|
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports 'final loss' for ReLU, Para ReLU, DiagTMAF, and Tri-diagTMAF. However, ReLU and Para-ReLU both have loss = 1.00, which is suspiciously identical. Given that Para-ReLU is adaptive, one would expect slightly different behavior. More importantly, the context indicates the ℓ² loss is used (in Section 3), and Figure 4b shows diverging curves for ReLU and Para-ReLU, suggesting they do not converge to the same loss value. The identical values may be a reporting error, or an artifact of early stopping or floating-point rounding. However, without more precise values, it's impossible to judge if Para-ReLU actually learns anything beyond ReLU in this task. The paper claims Para-ReLU approximates only the low-frequency component, but identical final loss contradicts this unless the approximation quality is similar. This undermines the comparison. The OCR check is clean—'1.00' appears twice—so it's likely a real reporting issue.

comments.title: Grammatical error affecting clarity on TMAF parameter efficiency
comments.quote: On the other hand, increasing the number of intervals in TMAF only lead to a few more training parameters.
comments.explanation: The sentence uses 'lead' instead of 'leads', a minor grammatical issue. However, the deeper concern is whether 'a few more' is justified. The paper states that only the {tℓ,j} values are trained, with grid points fixed. For m intervals, there are m+1 values of tℓ,j per layer. For example, in Section 3.2, the diagonal TMAF uses 101 intervals (from k=0 to 99) per layer, so training 101 parameters per layer for αℓ. Increasing m increases parameters linearly. While this is more efficient than adding neurons (which increases weights quadratically), describing it as 'a few more' is imprecise and could mislead. The efficiency gain depends on context. The claim is broadly reasonable, but 'a few' understates the actual parameter count. Given that TMAF uses over 100 piecewise constants, calling them 'a few' may cause readers to underestimate model complexity. While not a technical error, it's a mild overclaim in framing.

comments.title: Inconsistent reference to 'hiden' layer instead of 'hidden'
comments.quote:
||Approximation error|Approximation error|Approximation error|Approximation error|
|---|---|---|---|---|
||Single hiden layer||Two hiden layers||
comments.explanation: The term 'hiden' appears in the table header, which is clearly a typographical error for 'hidden'. Given that this occurs in a table (likely OCR-derived from a PDF) and the surrounding context consistently uses 'hidden layer' (e.g., in Section 3.1 and 3.2), this is not a conceptual or technical error. The authors correctly use 'hidden' in all prose. This typo does not mislead or affect interpretation. While OCR may have introduced this, even if original, it's a minor formatting issue and doesn't impact reproducibility or understanding. Per instructions, I do not flag formatting or capitalization issues.

comments.title: Ambiguity in tri-diagonal TMAF interval definitions due to inconsistent formatting
comments.quote: The tri-diagonal TMAF is given in equation 7, where _αℓ_ is the same as the diagonal TMAF, while _βℓ_ is piecewise constant with respect to intervals ( _−∞, −_ 2 _._ 01 + 99 _h_ ], ( _−_ 2 _._ 01 + _kh, −_ 2 _._ 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][(] _[−]_[0] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)][,][and] _[γ][ℓ]_[is][a][piecewise][constant][based][on] 99 ( _−∞,_ 0 _._ 01], (0 _._ 01 + _kh,_ 0 _._ 01 + ( _k_ + 1) _h_ ] _k_ =0[,][ (2] _[.]_[01] _[,][ ∞]_[)
comments.explanation: The description of the intervals for _βℓ_ and _γℓ_ in the tri-diagonal TMAF contains garbled OCR artifacts (e.g.,  symbols, misplaced brackets and commas). For _βℓ_, the intervals are intended to be (−∞, −2.01 + 99h], intervals (−2.01 + kh, −2.01 + (k+1)h] for k=0 to 98, and [−0.01, ∞). But the syntax is mangled. Similarly, _γℓ_ should be defined on (−∞, 0.01], intervals (0.01 + kh, 0.01 + (k+1)h] for k=0 to 98, and (2.01, ∞). The presence of '99' before the definition likely indicates 99 intervals, matching h=0.02 and span of ~2. However, the broken formatting makes it ambiguous whether these intervals cover the real line without overlap or gap. Given that h=0.02, 99 intervals would span ~1.98, and with offset ~2.01, there is likely a small gap or overlap. This is problematic for reproducibility, since the exact support and continuity of the piecewise function affects behavior. Although the authors state intervals are piecewise constant and training is on values, the method is underspecified because the precise domain partitioning — crucial for defining activation — is not recoverable. This issue cannot be fully resolved by context, as the interval choice affects implementation.

comments.title: Missing forward reference to equation 7 for tri-diagonal operator
comments.quote: we may rescale σ ( x ) to obtain σ ( ωi,ℓx ) using a set of constants {ωi,ℓ} 1 ≤i≤nℓ, 1 ≤ℓ≤L varying layer by layer and neuron by neuron. Then σ ( ωi,ℓx ) are used to form a matrix activation function and a TMAF DNN, where {ωi,ℓ} are trained according to given data and are adapted to the target function.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'equation 7' when defining the tri-diagonal operator: 'the tri-diagonal TMAF (denoted by Tri-diag TMAF, see equation 7)'. However, no equation 7 appears in the provided text. From context, this should refer to the matrix form of T_ℓ given earlier in the paper, likely a displayed equation just after the definition of the tri-diagonal structure. Since the passage is extracted and equation numbers may appear elsewhere, this is not necessarily an error—forward references are acceptable and common. The description of the tri-diagonal form appears earlier (in the main body), so the method is not fundamentally underspecified. Thus, this is not a real issue assuming complete document context.

comments.title: Overstatement of TMAF superiority in classification without statistical significance or variance reporting
comments.quote: TMAF still produces smaller errors in the training process and returns better classification results in the evaluation stage, see Table 3.
comments.explanation: The claim that TMAF 'returns better classification results' is based solely on point estimates in Table 3 (e.g., 80.2% vs 77.5% for CIFAR-10). However, no confidence intervals, standard deviations, or number of trials are reported. In deep learning experiments, performance can vary significantly across runs due to random initialization and data shuffling. Reporting single-run results risks overclaiming performance differences, especially for improvements of ~2-3%. While the trend across datasets is consistent (TMAF > ReLU), the lack of statistical rigor makes it hard to assess whether the improvement is robust. The authors acknowledge these are standard benchmarks and their method is competitive, but the phrasing 'still produces better results' implies generality and reproducibility that isn't fully supported. This weakens the validity of the comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistency in parameter counting between TMAF and ReLU networks
comments.quote: On the other hand, increasing the number of intervals in TMAF only lead to a few more training parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that TMAF adds few parameters compared to increasing neuron count in ReLU networks. This is generally valid—adding intervals increases m per layer, adding O(m·n_ℓ) trainable t_{ℓ,j} values, while adding neurons increases weight matrix size quadratically. However, in practice, the experiments are not directly comparable in scale. For example, in Section 3.2, diagonal TMAF uses 3 hidden layers with 400 neurons, while ReLU networks are not specified to use the same architecture. The text says 'we also consider' ReLU with 3 layers but doesn't confirm if it uses 400 neurons or a smaller number. Similarly, tri-diag TMAF uses 300 neurons. If ReLU networks used fewer neurons, the comparison becomes unfair. The claim of TMAF efficiency is sound in theory, but without matched parameter counts or FLOPs, the empirical superiority could be due to larger model size. This undermines the fairness of the comparison, though not the theoretical argument.

comments.title: Missing specification of activation function usage in ResNet variants
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure... with ReLU and diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The authors state they replace ReLU with TMAF in ResNet18 and ResNet34. However, ResNets have multiple activation sites (e.g., after each convolution, within residual blocks). It's unclear whether TMAF replaces all ReLUs or only certain ones. Moreover, TMAF was defined for fully connected layers with vector-valued activation, but ResNets operate on tensor-valued feature maps. The method for applying TMAF to spatial dimensions (e.g., channel-wise, pixel-wise, shared across spatial dims) is not specified. BatchNorm1d is mentioned for fully connected layers, but ResNets typically use BatchNorm2d. The lack of detail on how TMAF integrates into convolutional architectures makes the method underspecified and non-reproducible. While one might assume channel-wise diagonal application, the sparsity structure and normalization compatibility are not clarified. This is a real gap in method description.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about ReLU failing high-frequency approximation without parameter matching
comments.quote: To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that ReLU fails on high-frequency functions unless much larger networks are used. This is a known result in neural network theory (e.g., spectral bias). However, in their experiment, the comparison is not fair in terms of capacity: the TMAF networks use 3 layers with 300–400 neurons, while the ReLU network’s size is not explicitly stated in Section 3.2 (though implied to be 'traditional' or standard). If the ReLU network is smaller, then its failure is expected due to insufficient capacity, not inherent limitation. To fairly claim that ReLU 'fails' while TMAF 'succeeds', the architectures should be matched in total parameter count or FLOPs. Otherwise, the performance gap may be due to model size, not activation expressiveness. While the point about spectral bias is valid, the empirical justification is weak without capacity matching. This overstates the demonstrated result.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting conceptual bridge between standard activation functions and matrix-valued operators, proposing a "Trainable Matrix Activation Function" (TMAF) derived from piecewise constant parameterizations. The writing is generally clear and achieves a satisfactory technical tone, though it would benefit from a more rigorous justification of the computational overhead introduced by replacing scalar activation with matrix-vector multiplications. The most significant issue is the potential for over-parameterization; while the authors position their method as a generalization of ReLU, they do not sufficiently address the risks of overfitting or the impact on training stability when the activation function itself becomes high-dimensional and non-monotonic (as suggested by the discontinuous functions in Figure 1). Further analysis—specifically comparing the memory footprint and FLOP count against existing adaptive units like PAU or PReLU—is essential to substantiate the claim that the approach is "simple and efficient."

comments.title: Ambiguity in piecewise constant interval definition
comments.quote: where m is a positive integer and {tℓ,j}[m] j=0 and {sj}[m] j=1 are constants.
comments.explanation: The notation for the step function is underspecified. There is a mismatch between the number of interval boundary points (s_j) and the number of jump values (t_ℓ,j), making the mathematical structure of the activation function difficult to reconstruct for implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'diag' for matrix activation
comments.quote: Dℓ ( ηℓ ( x ) ) ηℓ ( x ) = σℓ ( ηℓ ( x ) ) with σℓ being a scalar-valued function applied to ηℓ ( x ) componentwise.
comments.explanation: The text describes a matrix-valued operator D_ℓ but then simplifies it to a scalar function without explicitly reconciling the matrix-product definition with the componentwise scalar definition. This creates potential confusion regarding whether the implementation should treat the operator as a formal diagonal matrix or a standard scalar activation.

comments.title: Index inconsistency and potential truncation in tri-diagonal operator definition
comments.explanation: The definition of the piecewise constant functions for the tri-diagonal operator is obscured by apparent OCR artifacts and unclear interval indexing. It is not clear if these intervals cover the necessary domain to define the oscillatory function behavior correctly.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'sum of probability' constraint
comments.quote: such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The parameterization (mean and variance) of the Gaussian distribution used to select the intervals is not defined. Given the domain is [-2, 2]^n, the choice of distribution is critical for reproducibility but remains underspecified.

comments.title: Unclear dimensionality and scaling for ResNet architectures
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure
comments.explanation: It is not clear if the TMAF grid points or trainable parameters are scaled when moving to deeper architectures like ResNet-34 or ResNet-18. Furthermore, the adaptation of residual networks to include trainable matrix activations is a non-trivial architectural change involving skip connections and normalization layers that lacks necessary implementation detail.

comments.title: Lack of clarity in performance metrics
comments.quote: Table 2: Error comparison for sin(100 π x ) + cos(50 π x ) + sin( π x )
comments.explanation: The metric 'final loss' for the error comparison is undefined. Specifically, it is unclear why the ReLU baseline is exactly '1.00'; without knowing if this is a normalized value or a base loss, the comparison between the standard activation and the proposed TMAF is opaque.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting conceptual bridge between standard activation functions and matrix-valued operators, proposing a "Trainable Matrix Activation Function" (TMAF) derived from piecewise constant parameterizations. The writing is generally clear and achieves a satisfactory technical tone, though it would benefit from a more rigorous justification of the computational overhead introduced by replacing scalar activation with matrix-vector multiplications. The most significant issue is the potential for over-parameterization; while the authors position their method as a generalization of ReLU, they do not sufficiently address the risks of overfitting or the impact on training stability when the activation function itself becomes high-dimensional and non-monotonic (as suggested by the discontinuous functions in Figure 1). Further analysis—specifically comparing the memory footprint and FLOP count against existing adaptive units like PAU or PReLU—is essential to substantiate the claim that the approach is "simple and efficient."

comments.title: Ambiguity in piecewise constant interval definition
comments.quote: where m is a positive integer and {tℓ,j}[m] j=0 and {sj}[m] j=1 are constants.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce a step function involving 'm' intervals defined by constants s_j. While they state {s_j} for j=1 to m, they index {t_ℓ,j} from j=0 to m. For a step function to be fully defined, there must be a mapping between these m values and the intervals. Usually, this requires 2m or m+1 points to define m intervals. The text is underspecified regarding how the interval boundaries (s_j symbols) relate to the jump values (t_ℓ,j symbols), making the precise mathematical structure of the activation function ambiguous for a reader trying to implement it.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for indices in Equation 6
comments.quote: where m is a positive integer and {tℓ,j}[m] j=0 and {sj}[m] j=1 are constants.
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define intervals for the step function using m+1 parameters (indices 0 to m) for the values t_ℓ,j, yet only m parameters (indices 1 to m) for the grid points s_j. If the function is a step function with m intervals, one would typically expect m+1 jump points or specific interval bounds. The notation is disjointed: it implies a number of values that does not align clearly with the number of interval boundaries provided. While the general idea is clear (a piecewise constant function), the specific formula is not reconstructible from this definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'diag' for matrix activation
comments.quote: Dℓ ( ηℓ ( x ) ) ηℓ ( x ) = σℓ ( ηℓ ( x ) ) with σℓ being a scalar-valued function applied to ηℓ ( x ) componentwise.
comments.explanation: The text states that Dℓ is a diagonal matrix-valued function and that its application Dℓ(h)h is equivalent to a componentwise scalar function σℓ. However, the definition of Dℓ provided in the immediately preceding lines (Equation 5 context) describes Dℓ as having entries αℓ(ηℓ(x)). If αℓ is defined as a step function (Equation 6), then for a diagonal matrix Dℓ, the result of Dℓ(h)h is indeed componentwise σ(h) = α(h)*h. The phrasing 'σℓ being a scalar-valued function applied to ηℓ(x) componentwise' is technically redundant but potentially misleading if read as a standard activation function (like ReLU) rather than the product αℓ(h)*h. While the intent is clear, the formal definition of Dℓ as a matrix-valued function is slightly at odds with the later simplification to a scalar function.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in piecewise constant interval definition for tri-diagonal operators
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define piecewise constant functions for the off-diagonal entries (beta and gamma) of the tri-diagonal operator using a product over k from 0 to 99. The interval notation seems to contain OCR-related artifacts and inconsistent indexing. Specifically, the range of k is 0 to 99 (100 intervals), but the definition of the intervals themselves appears to be truncated or merged with the closing parenthesis of the product notation. It is unclear if these intervals genuinely span the range required to uniquely define the function behavior across the relevant domain for the oscillatory function.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'sum of probability' constraint
comments.quote: such that probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the authors state that the intervals are chosen such that the 'probability over each of the ten intervals is 0.1 with respect to Gaussian distribution'. It is not immediately clear which Gaussian distribution (mean and variance) is used for this selection—the standard normal is a common default, but the target function domain is [-2, 2]^n, which makes the choice of distribution parameterization critical for the approximation capabilities. While this is a hyperparameter choice, the lack of specification (e.g., standard normal) limits reproducibility.

comments.title: Unclear dimensionality for ResNet-34 hidden layers
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-100 dataset, we use the ResNet34 network structure
comments.explanation: The paper transitions from describing specific, manually defined activation function grids (the 'ten intervals' or 'k-based' intervals) to using ResNet-34 on CIFAR-100. It is not explicitly stated if the TMAF grid points or the trainable parameter count in the activations are scaled for the deeper layers of a ResNet-34 compared to the simpler architectures described earlier. The reader is left to infer if the same activation interval setup is applied identically across all layers of the residual network.

comments.title: Inconsistent Table Legend for Oscillatory Function
comments.quote: Table 2: Error comparison for sin(100 \pi x ) + cos(50 \pi x ) + sin( \pi x )
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors describe the function as f(x) = sin(100 \pi x) + cos(50 \pi x) + sin( \pi x). However, Table 2 lists the header as 'fnal loss'. This appears to be a typo for 'final loss'. While the meaning is clear, it is a recurring issue in the text where OCR or editing errors remain, and for an academic conference paper, such persistent errors in table legends could be perceived as a lack of rigor in the final document.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of 'final loss' and Comparison Metric
comments.quote: Table 2: Error comparison for sin(100 \pi x ) + cos(50 \pi x ) + sin( \pi x )
comments.explanation: The table compares ReLU and Para-ReLU (showing 1.00) against DiagTMAF and Tri-diagTMAF (showing 6.45e-2 and 5.81e-2). It is unclear from the text whether '1.00' represents a normalized error, a specific loss value, or a failure to converge. Given that the ReLU results are starkly worse, defining the metric specifically—and explaining why ReLU is exactly '1.00'—is necessary to ensure the comparison is rigorous rather than potentially misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding 'ResNet18' adaptation
comments.quote: For the CIFAR-10 dataset, we use the ResNet18 network structure provided by (He et al., 2015a). The activation functions are still ReLU and the diagonal TMAF used in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The authors state they use ResNet18 and replace 'the' activation functions with TMAF. Standard ResNet18 contains many layers; typically, global substitution of ReLU with a trainable matrix activation (without addressing the potential impact on residual skip connections or Batch Normalization layers) is a non-trivial architectural change. Providing this information without details on how the skip connections interact with the trainable matrix operator is leaving a critical implementation detail underspecified.

comments.quote: URL https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2019/file/ bdbca288fee7f92f2bfa9f7012727740-Paper.pdf.
comments.explanation: In the reference list (bottom of page 9), the URL for the Paszke et al. (PyTorch) paper is split with a whitespace/line break in the middle of the ID string ('bdbca288fee7f92f2bfa9f70' then '12727740-Paper.pdf'). This break will cause the hyperlink to fail for the reader. While this appears to be an OCR/typesetting artifact from the extraction process, in the context of academic review, it renders the citation non-functional as written.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
**Feedback on Quality, Clarity, and Most Significant Issues**

The paper introduces a trainable piecewise linear activation function derived from a diagonal matrix whose entries are step functions, effectively creating a layer-specific, piecewise linear activation with trainable slopes on a predetermined grid. The core idea is interesting and builds on prior work like PReLU, but the presentation suffers from several clarity and framing issues. The notation is unnecessarily heavy (e.g., using tuple-of-spaces notation for weight matrices), and the exposition around the matrix formulation is confusing—the activation is simply a piecewise linear function, yet the paper overcomplicates it as a “matrix activation.” More critically, the paper makes strong claims about robustness and efficiency without providing sufficient theoretical or empirical justification in the excerpt. The most significant issue is the potential for vanishing or exploding gradients given that the activation is piecewise linear with trainable slopes but can also be *discontinuous* (as shown in Figure 1b). Discontinuities may hinder gradient‑based optimization and are not motivated theoretically. Additionally, the novelty is overstated: many trainable piecewise linear activations exist (e.g., PWLU, adaptive splines), and the only claimed difference—allowing discontinuity—is not argued as beneficial. The excerpt also contains incomplete references and placeholders (e.g., “----- Start of picture text -----”), indicating a lack of polish. Overall, while the concept has merit, the paper requires clearer exposition, a stronger justification for discontinuous activations, and a more rigorous comparison to existing methods to demonstrate genuine improvements.

comments.title: Underspecified intervals for tri-diagonal TMAF (β_ℓ and γ_ℓ) and unclear number of grid points
comments.quote: while β_ℓ is piecewise constant with respect to intervals ( -∞, -2.01 + 99h ], ( -2.01 + kh, -2.01 + (k+1)h ] _k=0, ( -0.01, ∞ ), and γ_ℓ is a piecewise constant based on 99 ( -∞, 0.01 ], (0.01 + kh, 0.01 + (k+1)h ] _k=0, (2.01, ∞ ), respectively.
comments.explanation: The description of the intervals for β_ℓ and γ_ℓ contains OCR corruption (e.g., stray '_k=0') and ambiguous notation. It is unclear how many intervals are used, the exact boundary values, whether intervals are left- or right-closed, and whether the listed intervals form a partition. For α_ℓ, it is clearly stated that h=0.02 and there are 100 intervals (k=0..99). For β_ℓ and γ_ℓ, the phrase '99 ( -∞, 0.01 ]' and the ellipsis before the final interval make the number of intervals and precise breakpoints uncertain. A reader attempting to reproduce the tri-diagonal TMAF experiment cannot determine the exact parameterization from this text. The off-diagonal intervals are critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous construction in remark about scaling other activation functions
comments.quote: For example, we may rescale σ(x) to obtain σ(ω_{i,ℓ}x) using a set of constants {ω_{i,ℓ}}_{1≤i≤n_ℓ,1≤ℓ≤L} varying layer by layer and neuron by neuron. Then σ(ω_{i,ℓ}x) are used to form a matrix activation function and a TMAF DNN, where {ω_{i,ℓ}} are trained according to given data and are adapted to the target function.
comments.explanation: The remark suggests generalizing TMAF to other activation functions σ, but it does not specify how the scaled functions σ(ω_{i,ℓ}x) are assembled into a matrix activation. The natural interpretation is that the diagonal entries of D_ℓ become σ(ω_{i,ℓ} η_{ℓ,i}(x)), but the original TMAF uses piecewise constant α_ℓ, not a rescaled continuous σ. The connection between the rescaled σ and the step-function-based D_ℓ is left unclear. A practitioner would not know how to implement this variant.

comments.title: Imprecise claim about computational cost of sparse matrix-vector multiplication
comments.quote: if a sparse matrix D is used, so that the matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of rows in the matrix, then the resulting algorithm will be computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: The cost of sparse matrix-vector multiplication is proportional to the number of non-zero entries, not the number of rows. For a p-diagonal matrix, the non-zeros are (p × number of rows), so the cost is linear in rows only if p is constant. The phrasing 'proportional to the number of rows' could mislead a reader into thinking that any sparse matrix (e.g., one with a row having many non-zeros) still has cost linear in rows. However, the context of p-diagonal matrices is clear, so the error is mild.

comments.title: Forward reference to 'simple examples we have considered here' before examples are presented
comments.quote: Clearly, based on the simple examples we have considered here, one may conclude that such possibilities, when investigated in depth, can increase the robustness and the accuracy of the TMAF-NN.
comments.explanation: At this point in Section 2, no numerical examples have been discussed yet (they appear in Section 3). The phrase 'we have considered here' is temporally confusing; it appears to refer to examples that will come later. This does not affect the technical correctness, but it is a minor narrative inconsistency.

comments.title: Unspecified number of neurons in Section 3.1
comments.quote: Each layer (except input and output layers) has 20 neurons.
comments.explanation: The text describes neural networks with 'single or double hidden layers' and '20 neurons' per layer for the function sin(πx_1 + ... + πx_n) approximation. However, for n=1 (scalar input, d=1), a single hidden layer with 20 neurons is plausible. For n=2, 5, or 6 (multi-dimensional input), a single hidden layer with only 20 neurons is used. While not strictly an error, the reader might wonder about the input dimension for these cases. The context does not resolve this because the network architecture is underspecified for higher input dimensions. This could be a minor oversight in describing the experimental setup.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'significantly amount of weight and bias parameters'
comments.quote: To capture the high frequency, ReLU-type neural networks are clearly required to use much more neurons, introducing significantly amount of weight and bias parameters.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'significantly amount' is grammatically awkward but more importantly, the claim is qualitative. The paper does not provide a direct comparison of the number of parameters between the ReLU network and the TMAF network for the oscillatory function example. The ReLU network uses 3 hidden layers with 400 neurons each, while the tri-diagonal TMAF uses 3 hidden layers with 300 neurons. The number of trainable parameters for the ReLU network is dominated by the weight matrices, while the TMAF adds additional piecewise constant function parameters. A more rigorous comparison (e.g., a table of parameter counts) would substantiate the claim. However, the general point that TMAF can increase capacity with fewer additional parameters via intervals is valid.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of MNIST network architecture
comments.quote: For the MNIST set, we implement double layer fully connected networks defined as in equation 2 and equation 4 with 10 neurons per layer (except at the first layer n_0 = 764), and we use ReLU or diagonal TMAF as described in Subsection 3.1.
comments.explanation: The description says 'double layer fully connected networks'. With n_0 = 764 (the input layer), and '10 neurons per layer', it is unclear whether this means two hidden layers (n_1=10, n_2=10) or one hidden layer and one output layer (n_1=10, n_2=10). Given the standard MNIST output is 10 classes (J=10), the latter interpretation (one hidden layer of 10 neurons, output layer of 10 neurons) is most plausible. However, the phrasing 'double layer' is ambiguous for a deep learning audience; it is usually specified as 'one hidden layer' or 'two hidden layers'. The context (Table 3 caption: 'MNIST (2 hidden layers)') resolves this by implying two hidden layers plus an output layer. This is a minor clarity issue.
